stem: Solve the separable equation \( \dfrac{dy}{dx} = [[k]]y \), \( y(0) = [[y0]] \), and evaluate the solution at \( x = [[t0]] \).
solution: Separating variables, \( \dfrac{dy}{y} = [[k]]\,dx \), so \( \ln|y| = [[k]]x + C \) and \( y(x) = C_1 e^{[[k]]x} \). The condition \( y(0) = [[y0]] \) gives \( C_1 = [[y0]] \), so \( y([[t0]]) = [[y0]]e^{[[k]]\cdot[[t0]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses the wrong sign in the exponent.
This treats the growth as linear rather than exponential.
This multiplies the rate and time outside of an exponential.
This drops the initial value \( [[y0]] \), leaving \( C_1 = 1 \).
This is one more than the correct value.
This is one less than the correct value.

stem: Solve the separable equation \( \dfrac{dy}{dx} = [[k]]xy \), \( y(0) = [[y0]] \), and evaluate the solution at \( x = [[t0]] \).
solution: Separating variables, \( \dfrac{dy}{y} = [[k]]x\,dx \), so \( \ln|y| = \dfrac{[[k]]x^{2}}{2} + C \), giving \( y(x) = C_1 e^{[[k]]x^{2}/2} \). Since \( y(0) = [[y0]] \), \( C_1 = [[y0]] \), so \( y([[t0]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This drops the factor of \( \tfrac12 \) obtained when integrating \( [[k]]x \).
This treats the equation as \( y' = [[k]]y \), ignoring the factor of \( x \).
This forgets to square \( [[t0]] \) inside the exponent.
This flips the sign of the exponent.
This is one more than the correct value.
This is one less than the correct value.

stem: Solve the nonlinear separable equation \( \dfrac{dy}{dx} = [[k]]y^{2} \), \( y(0) = [[y0]] \), and evaluate the solution at \( x = [[t0]] \).
solution: Separating variables, \( \dfrac{dy}{y^{2}} = [[k]]\,dx \), so \( -\dfrac{1}{y} = [[k]]x + C \). The condition \( y(0) = [[y0]] \) gives \( C = -\dfrac{1}{[[y0]]} \), so \( y(x) = \dfrac{[[y0]]}{1-[[k]][[y0]]x} \). At \( x = [[t0]] \), \( y = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This mistakes the equation for the LINEAR \( y' = [[k]]y \), giving exponential growth instead of the correct rational blow-up form.
This uses the wrong sign in the denominator.
This inverts the rational solution, multiplying instead of dividing.
This uses a single Euler step (a linear approximation) instead of the exact solution.
This is one more than the correct value.
This is one less than the correct value.

stem: The curve \( y = y(x) \), with \( y > 0 \), satisfies the separable equation \( y\,\dfrac{dy}{dx} = [[k]]x \), \( y(0) = [[y0]] \). Evaluate \( y([[t0]]) \), taking the positive branch.
solution: Separating gives \( y\,dy = [[k]]x\,dx \), so \( \dfrac{y^{2}}{2} = \dfrac{[[k]]x^{2}}{2} + C \). The condition \( y(0) = [[y0]] \) gives \( C = \dfrac{[[y0]]^{2}}{2} \), so \( y(x)^{2} = [[y0]]^{2}+[[k]]x^{2} \). Taking the positive branch, \( y([[t0]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This flips the sign of \( k \) inside the radical.
This forgets to square \( x \) before scaling by \( k \).
This forgets to square \( y_0 \) before adding under the radical.
This takes the negative branch instead of the positive one.
This is one more than the correct value.
This is one less than the correct value.

stem: The curve \( y = y(x) \), with \( y > 0 \), satisfies \( \dfrac{dy}{dx} = \dfrac{\cos x}{y} \), \( y(0) = [[y0]] \). Evaluate \( y\!\left(\dfrac{[[m]]\pi}{2}\right) \), taking the positive branch.
solution: Separating gives \( y\,dy = \cos x\,dx \), so \( \dfrac{y^{2}}{2} = \sin x + C \). Since \( y(0) = [[y0]] \) and \( \sin 0 = 0 \), \( C = \dfrac{[[y0]]^{2}}{2} \), so \( y(x)^{2} = [[y0]]^{2}+2\sin x \). At \( x = \dfrac{[[m]]\pi}{2} \), taking the positive branch, \( y = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This flips the sign of the sine term inside the radical.
This forgets to double the sine term and to take a square root.
This drops the initial-value term entirely.
This takes the negative branch instead of the positive one.
This is one more than the correct value.
This is one less than the correct value.

stem: The curve \( y = y(x) \), with \( y > 0 \), satisfies \( \dfrac{dy}{dx} = \dfrac{e^{x}}{y} \), \( y(0) = [[y0]] \). Evaluate \( y([[t0]]) \), taking the positive branch.
solution: Separating gives \( y\,dy = e^{x}\,dx \), so \( \dfrac{y^{2}}{2} = e^{x} + C \). Since \( y(0) = [[y0]] \), \( \dfrac{[[y0]]^{2}}{2} = 1 + C \), giving \( C = \dfrac{[[y0]]^{2}}{2}-1 \), so \( y(x)^{2} = 2e^{x}+[[y0]]^{2}-2 \). At \( x = [[t0]] \), taking the positive branch, \( y = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets to double the exponential term while still shifting the constant.
This forgets the constant shift of \( -2 \) needed to match the initial condition.
This forgets to take the square root of the implicit relation.
This takes the negative branch instead of the positive one.
This is one more than the correct value.
This is one less than the correct value.

stem: A cooling object's temperature \( T(t) \) obeys Newton's law of cooling \( \dfrac{dT}{dt} = -[[k]](T-[[tenv]]) \), a separable equation, where \( [[tenv]] \) is the constant ambient temperature. If \( T(0) = [[t0v]] \), find \( T([[t0]]) \).
solution: Separating variables, \( \dfrac{dT}{T-[[tenv]]} = -[[k]]\,dt \), so \( T(t) = [[tenv]] + Ce^{-[[k]]t} \). Since \( T(0) = [[t0v]] \), \( C = [[t0v]]-[[tenv]] \), so \( T([[t0]]) = [[tenv]]+([[t0v]]-[[tenv]])e^{-[[k]]\cdot[[t0]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses the wrong sign in the exponent, giving heating instead of cooling toward the ambient temperature.
This ignores the ambient temperature shift entirely.
This flips the sign of the transient term, undershooting instead of approaching \( [[tenv]] \) correctly.
This treats the decay as linear rather than exponential.
This is one more than the correct value.
This is one less than the correct value.

stem: A quantity \( y(t) \), with \( 0 < y < 1 \), grows according to the logistic equation \( \dfrac{dy}{dt} = [[a]]y(1-y) \), \( y(0) = \dfrac{[[num]]}{[[den]]} \). Evaluate \( y([[t0]]) \).
solution: By partial fractions, \( \int\left(\dfrac1y+\dfrac1{1-y}\right)dy = \ln\left|\dfrac{y}{1-y}\right| = [[a]]t+C \), so \( \dfrac{y}{1-y} = Ae^{[[a]]t} \) with \( A = \dfrac{y_0}{1-y_0} = [[A]] \). Solving for \( y \), \( y(t) = \dfrac{Ae^{[[a]]t}}{1+Ae^{[[a]]t}} \), so \( y([[t0]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This ignores the saturation term \( (1-y) \), treating the growth as plain exponential.
This uses the wrong sign in the exponent, growing toward the wrong limit.
This forgets to normalize by the denominator \( 1+Ae^{at} \).
This places \( A \) in the numerator instead of \( Ae^{at} \).
This is one more than the correct value.
This is one less than the correct value.
